Category: Hospitality & Tourism: Hotels, Tourism, Travel Agencies, Food & Beverage
Description: Hidden risks in booking engines, POS, and CRM tools.
Prerequisites: A basic familiarity with common hospitality software such as booking engines, point-of-sale systems, or CRM tools is helpful, though no technical or AI background is required to follow this course.
Learning objectives: By the end of this course, you will be able to identify the hidden risks embedded within AI-powered booking engines, point-of-sale systems, and CRM tools commonly used across the hospitality industry. You will also be able to explain how vendor AI operates within these platforms and apply practical strategies to assess and manage the associated risks within your organisation.

lessons.Description: Many hospitality businesses rely on booking engines, point-of-sale systems, and CRM tools that now include built-in AI features, often without staff realising it. These vendor-supplied AI tools can make automatic decisions about pricing, customer communications, and data handling, which can introduce risks that are easy to overlook. This lesson explores how AI is quietly embedded within the systems your team already uses every day, and what that means for your business. You will learn to identify where these hidden AI features sit, understand the potential risks they carry, and feel more confident asking the right questions of your technology suppliers. No prior knowledge of AI is required, just a willingness to look more closely at the tools your business depends on.
lessons.content: Hidden risks in booking engines, POS, and CRM tools.

slides.subtitle: Risks hiding in your everyday tools
slides.slidecontent:
• AI hides in familiar tools
• Booking engines, POS, CRM
• Risks often go unnoticed
• Staff rarely question defaults
• Small gaps, big consequences

slides.subtitle: What lies beneath the surface
slides.slidecontent:
• Booking engines often use AI to adjust pricing and availability without alerting hotel staff
• Decisions made in real time, behind the scenes
• Guests may see different prices based on browsing history, location, or device type
• Opaque algorithms make it difficult to audit why a rate was offered or declined
• Staff rarely have visibility into the logic driving these automated choices
• Small configuration errors can trigger widespread pricing or availability mistakes

slides.subtitle: Where convenience conceals compliance concerns
slides.slidecontent:
• AI suggests prices automatically
• Guest data leaves your system
• Consent settings often hidden
• Bias baked into algorithms
• Audits rarely happen

slides.subtitle: Where automation can quietly go wrong
slides.slidecontent:
• Prices update without warning
• Wrong rooms get confirmed
• Overbooking happens silently
• Discounts misfire automatically
• Guest data gets mismatched

slides.subtitle: Where AI hides in plain sight
slides.slidecontent:
• Booking engines suggest prices automatically
• Chatbots answer guest queries
• CRM tools predict customer behaviour
• POS flags unusual transactions
• Loyalty schemes personalise rewards

slides.subtitle: Warning signs before contracts are signed.
slides.slidecontent:
• Vague claims like "AI-powered" with no explanation of what the system actually does
• No data transparency
• Vendor unable to explain how guest data is used or stored after processing
• Pricing that changes based on "model updates" you have no control over
• No opt-out or override options for staff when the AI makes a decision
• References and case studies that only show best-case results, never limitations

slides.subtitle: Practical steps to minimise AI exposure
slides.slidecontent:
• Ask vendors directly which features use AI and what data those features access
• Review your contracts for clauses about data sharing with third parties
• Audit guest data regularly
• Train staff to recognise when AI-generated suggestions may be inaccurate or biased
• Limit the personal data fields connected to AI-powered tools where possible
• Keep a simple log of which systems use AI and when those systems were last reviewed

slides.subtitle: Core principles to carry forward always
slides.slidecontent:
• Vendor AI is already embedded in the tools your team uses every day
• Always ask suppliers what data their AI collects and how it is used
• Audit regularly
• Booking engines, POS, and CRM systems each carry their own hidden risks
• Staff awareness is your first and most practical line of defence
• Start small by reviewing one vendor contract this week for AI-related clauses

scripts.script: Let's start by thinking about the tools your team uses every single day. Your booking engine, your point of sale system, your CRM. These are familiar, trusted parts of how your property operates. But here's the thing: many of these tools now have AI built quietly into them, and most people never even notice. It's not hidden in a sinister way, it's just that vendors add these features as standard, and staff get on with their jobs without ever questioning what's happening underneath. And that's where the risk creeps in. Not through some dramatic system failure, but through small, overlooked gaps. A default setting nobody changed. An automated decision nobody reviewed. On their own, these things seem minor. But over time, they can add up to something much more significant, whether that's a compliance issue, a guest experience problem, or a reputational concern. So throughout this lesson, we're going to shine a light on exactly where those risks are hiding.

Let's start by looking at booking engines, because this is where a lot of hidden AI activity takes place. When a guest visits your website and searches for a room, there's often an algorithm working in the background, adjusting prices and availability in real time. The guest might see a completely different rate to someone else, based on things like their location, the device they're using, or even their browsing history. And here's the concern: your staff probably have no idea this is happening. There's no notification, no log to check, no easy way to ask the system why it offered a particular price or turned a booking away. These algorithms are largely opaque, meaning they're very difficult to audit or question. And if something is configured incorrectly, even a small setting in the wrong place, it can cause pricing or availability errors that affect a large number of guests before anyone notices. That's a significant operational risk.

So let's talk about booking engines, because this is where a lot of hotels first encounter vendor AI, and it's also where some of the biggest risks quietly creep in. When a booking engine uses AI to suggest prices, that sounds helpful, and it often is, but do you actually know what data it's using to make those decisions, or where that guest information is going once it leaves your property management system? In many cases, consent settings are buried deep in configuration menus that nobody ever looks at. And here's something worth thinking about: the algorithms powering these tools can carry bias, meaning certain guests may be shown different prices or options without anyone realising it. On top of all that, formal audits of how these systems behave are genuinely rare in our industry. So the convenience is real, but so are the compliance concerns, and that's exactly why we need to understand what's happening beneath the surface.

Now, booking engine AI can be genuinely impressive, but it does come with some real risks that are worth understanding. Because so much happens automatically, problems can creep in without anyone noticing straight away. For example, pricing algorithms can update rates in the background, sometimes at completely the wrong moment, and no one gets an alert. Room assignments can be confirmed incorrectly, so a guest arrives expecting one thing and gets another. Overbooking is another concern, because if the system is not syncing properly across channels, it can sell rooms that simply are not available. Discount rules can misfire too, applying the wrong rate to the wrong booking without any human ever reviewing it. And perhaps most seriously, guest data can get mixed up, meaning personal details or preferences end up attached to the wrong profile entirely. These are not hypothetical scenarios. They happen in real properties, and they tend to surface at the worst possible time, usually when a guest is standing right at the front desk.

So let's have a look at where AI is actually showing up in the systems you might already be using every day. When your booking engine automatically adjusts room rates based on demand, that is AI working in the background. When a guest visits your website and a chatbot answers their questions at two in the morning, that is AI too. Your CRM system might be quietly predicting which guests are likely to return, or flagging those who haven't visited in a while. Over at the point of sale, AI can spot transactions that look unusual and raise an alert before a problem escalates. And those loyalty programmes that seem to know exactly what reward to offer each member? That personalisation is driven by AI analysing behaviour and preferences. The point here is that AI is not some distant, futuristic concept. For many of you, it is already embedded in the tools you are working with right now.

Before you sign anything, there are some warning signs worth knowing about. If a vendor keeps saying their system is "AI-powered" but can't actually explain what that means in practice, that's a concern. You should also be asking questions about your guest data. Where does it go? Who can access it? If they can't answer clearly, that's a problem. Watch out for pricing structures that can shift whenever the vendor decides to update their model, because that can make budgeting very difficult. Another thing to look for is whether your staff can actually step in and override the system when needed. If the answer is no, that removes a really important layer of human judgement. And finally, be cautious of case studies that only ever show glowing results. Every technology has limitations, and a vendor who won't acknowledge theirs is one you should approach with real caution. These are the questions to ask before you commit.

So now let's look at what you can actually do to reduce your risk. The first thing is simply to ask your vendors directly which features use AI and what data those features are touching. A lot of hospitality businesses don't realise they can just ask that question. From there, it's worth reviewing your contracts to check whether your guest data is being shared with any third parties. You should also be auditing your guest data regularly, so you know exactly what you're holding and where it's going. On the staff side, make sure your team understands that AI suggestions are not always accurate, and they should feel confident questioning them. Where you can, try to limit how much personal data is connected to your AI-powered tools in the first place. And finally, keep a simple internal log of which systems use AI and when you last reviewed them. Small, consistent steps like these make a real difference over time.

So let's bring everything together. The most important thing to understand is that AI isn't some distant future technology. It's already sitting inside the systems your team logs into every single day. That means your responsibility starts now. Whenever you're speaking with a supplier, get into the habit of asking straightforward questions about what data their AI is collecting and what they're doing with it. Don't wait for something to go wrong before you look into this. Audit your systems regularly, and remember that your booking engine, your POS, and your CRM each come with their own set of risks that aren't always obvious at first glance. Your staff are genuinely your strongest line of defence here, so keeping them informed really does matter. And if you're not sure where to begin, here's a simple first step: pick one vendor contract this week and look for any clauses that mention AI or automated processing. That's all it takes to get started.
